revision.text:
'''Kurozumikyō''' (黒住教), literally "the Teachings of Kurozumi," is a [[Shinshukyo|Japanese New Religion]] largely derived from [[Shinto roots]] and founded in [[1846]]. The founder, a Shinto priest by the name of Kurozumi, claimed to have had in [[1814]] a divine union with [[Amaterasu]], goddess of the sun and chief goddess in the Shinto pantheon.
The core beliefs of the sect center around this idea, and the assertion that Amaterasu is the source of all light and life, and creator of all the universe. Mankind is believed to be able to tap into the divine power of Amaterasu in order to heal the sick and perform other miracles. The sect is based in [[Okayama]], and focuses on the virtues of sincerity, selflessness, hard work, and affirmation of the established social order.
Though Kurozumi's supposed divine experience occurred in 1814, the sect was not formally organized until 1846, when the priest and senior disciples assembled the ''Osadamegaki'', putting into writing all the beliefs, values, and laws of the sect. Originally, their religious and missionary activity was tolerated by the feudal Okayama lord, as it did not threaten his power or conflict greatly with the religious beliefs already practiced in the area.
By the time of the [[Meiji Restoration]] in [[1868]], the sect had gained followers in Kyushu and western and southwestern Honshu, all the way up to Tokyo. It gained independence from the Board of Shinto Affairs in 1876, and established its own Shinto shrine, the [[Munetada Shrine]], in Okayama in 1885. However, the Kurozumikyō sect failed to adapt as the nation modernized, and so it stagnated. As of 1978, the group claimed 218,000 followers.
==Reference== *Murakami Shigeyoshi (1985). "Kurozumikyō." <u>Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan</u>. Tokyo: Kodansha Ltd.
[[ja:黒住教]] [[Category:Shinshukyo|Kurozumikyo]] [[Category:Shinto|Kurozumikyo]]

The core beliefs of the sect center around this idea, and the assertion that Amaterasu is the source of all light and life, and creator of all the universe. Mankind is believed to be able to tap into the divine power of Amaterasu in order to heal the sick and perform other miracles. The sect is based in [[Okayama]], and focuses on the virtues of sincerity, selflessness, hard work, and affirmation of the established social order.
Though Kurozumi's supposed divine experience occurred in 1814, the sect was not formally organized until 1846, when the priest and senior disciples assembled the ''Osadamegaki'', putting into writing all the beliefs, values, and laws of the sect. Originally, their religious and missionary activity was tolerated by the feudal Okayama lord, as it did not threaten his power or conflict greatly with the religious beliefs already practiced in the area.
By the time of the [[Meiji Restoration]] in [[1868]], the sect had gained followers in Kyushu and western and southwestern Honshu, all the way up to Tokyo. It gained independence from the Board of Shinto Affairs in 1876, and established its own Shinto shrine, the [[Munetada Shrine]], in Okayama in 1885. However, the Kurozumikyō sect failed to adapt as the nation modernized, and so it stagnated. As of 1978, the group claimed 218,000 followers.
==Reference== *Murakami Shigeyoshi (1985). "Kurozumikyō." <u>Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan</u>. Tokyo: Kodansha Ltd.

'''Kurozumikyō''' (黒住教), literally "the Teachings of Kurozumi," is a [[Shinshūkyō|Japanese New Religion]] largely derived from [[Shinto]] roots and founded in 1846. The founder, a Shinto priest by the name of Kurozumi, claimed to have had a divine union with [[Amaterasu]], goddess of the sun and chief goddess in the Shinto pantheon, in 1814.
The core beliefs of the sect center around this idea, and the assertion that Amaterasu is the source of all light and life, and [[Creator deity|creator]] of all the universe. Mankind is believed to be able to tap into the divine power of Amaterasu in order to heal the sick and perform other miracles. The sect is based in [[Okayama]], and focuses on the virtues of sincerity, selflessness, hard work, and affirmation of the established social order.
Though Kurozumi's supposed divine experience occurred in 1814, the sect was not formally organized until 1846, when the priest and senior disciples assembled the ''Osadamegaki'', putting into writing all the beliefs, values, and laws of the sect. Originally, their religious and missionary activity was tolerated by the feudal Okayama lord, as it did not threaten his power or conflict greatly with the religious beliefs already practiced in the area.
By the time of the [[Meiji Restoration]] in 1868, the sect had gained followers in Kyushu and western and southwestern Honshū, all the way up to Tokyo. It gained independence from the Board of Shinto Affairs in 1876, and established its own Shinto shrine, the [[Munetada Shrine]], in Okayama in 1885. However, the Kurozumikyō sect failed to adapt as the nation modernized, and so it stagnated. As of 1978, the group claimed 218,000 followers.
==References== {{reflist}} {{no footnotes|date=December 2010}} * Murakami Shigeyoshi (1985). "Kurozumikyō." <u>Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan</u>. Tokyo: Kodansha Ltd. * [http://www.kurozumikyo.com/marukoto_e.html Marukoto: The Teaching of Roundness]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Kurozumikyo}} [[Category:Religious organisations based in Japan]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in 1846]] [[Category:Shinshukyo]] [[Category:Shinto]]

'''Kurozumikyō''' (黒住教), literally "the Teachings of Kurozumi," is a [[Japanese new religions|Japanese religion]] largely derived from [[Shinto]] roots and founded in 1846. The founder, a Shinto priest by the name of Kurozumi, had a Divine union with [[Amaterasu]], Goddess of the sun and chief Kami in the Shinto [[Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]], in 1814.
The core beliefs of the sect center around this idea, and the assertion that Amaterasu is the source of all light and life, and [[Creator deity|creator]] of all the universe. Mankind is believed to be able to tap into the divine power of Amaterasu in order to heal the sick and perform other miracles. The sect is based in [[Okayama]], and focuses on the virtues of sincerity, selflessness, hard work, and affirmation of the established social order.
Though Kurozumi's divine experience occurred in 1814, the sect was not formally organized until 1846, when the priest and senior disciples assembled the ''Osadamegaki'', putting into writing all the beliefs, values, and laws of the sect. Originally, their religious and missionary activity was tolerated by the feudal Okayama lord, as it did not threaten his power or conflict greatly with the religious beliefs already practiced in the area.
By the time of the [[Meiji Restoration]] in 1868, the sect had gained followers in Kyushu and western and southwestern Honshū, all the way up to Tokyo. It gained independence from the Board of Shinto Affairs in 1876, and established its own Shinto shrine, the [[Munetada Shrine]], in Okayama in 1885. However, the Kurozumikyō sect failed to adapt as the nation modernized, and so it stagnated. As of 1978, the group claimed 218,000 followers.
Muneharu Kurozumi, the Sixth Chief Patriarch of Kurozumikyō, is affiliated to the openly [[Historical revisionism (negationism)|revisionist]] lobby [[Nippon Kaigi]].<ref>Nippon Kaigi website</ref>

revision.text:
'''Kurozumikyō''' (黒住教), literally "the Teachings of Kurozumi," is a [[Japanese new religions|Japanese religion]] largely derived from [[Shinto]] roots and founded in 1846. The founder, a Shinto priest by the name of Kurozumi, had a Divine union with [[Amaterasu]], Goddess of the sun and chief Kami in the Shinto [[Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]], in 1814.
The core beliefs of the sect center around this idea, and the assertion that Amaterasu is the source of all light and life, and [[Creator deity|creator]] of all the universe. Mankind is believed to be able to tap into the divine power of Amaterasu in order to heal the sick and perform other miracles. The sect is based in [[Okayama]], and focuses on the virtues of sincerity, selflessness, hard work, and affirmation of the established social order.
Though Kurozumi's divine experience occurred in 1814, the sect was not formally organized until 1846, when the priest and senior disciples assembled the ''Osadamegaki'', putting into writing all the beliefs, values, and laws of the sect. Originally, their religious and missionary activity was tolerated by the feudal Okayama lord, as it did not threaten his power or conflict greatly with the religious beliefs already practiced in the area.
By the time of the [[Meiji Restoration]] in 1868, the sect had gained followers in Kyushu and western and southwestern Honshū, all the way up to Tokyo. It gained independence from the Board of Shinto Affairs in 1876, and established its own Shinto shrine, the [[Munetada Shrine]], in Okayama in 1885. However, the Kurozumikyō sect failed to adapt as the nation modernized, and so it stagnated. As of 1978, the group claimed 218,000 followers.
Muneharu Kurozumi, the Sixth Chief Patriarch of Kurozumikyō, is affiliated to the openly [[Historical revisionism (negationism)|revisionist]] lobby [[Nippon Kaigi]].<ref>Nippon Kaigi website</ref>
==References== {{reflist}} {{no footnotes|date=December 2010}} * Murakami Shigeyoshi (1985). "Kurozumikyō." <u>Kōdansha Encyclopedia of Japan</u>. Tokyo: Kōdansha Ltd.
==External links== * [http://www.kurozumikyo.com/marukoto_e.html Marukoto: The Teaching of Roundness]
{{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kurozumikyo}} [[Category:Religious organizations based in Japan]] [[Category:Religious organizations established in 1846]] [[Category:Shinshukyo]] [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:1846 establishments in Japan]]

Though Kurozumi's divine experience occurred in 1814, the sect was not formally organized until 1846, when the priest and senior disciples assembled the ''Osadamegaki'', putting into writing all the beliefs, values, and laws of the sect. Mankind is believed to be able to tap into the divine power of Amaterasu in order to heal the sick and perform other miracles. The sect is based in [[Okayama]], and focuses on the virtues of sincerity, selflessness, hard work, and affirmation of the established social order.
Though Kurozumi's divine experience occurred in 1814, the sect was not formally organized until 1846, when the priest and senior disciples assembled the ''Osadamegaki'', putting into writing all the beliefs, values, and laws of the sect. Originally, their religious and missionary activity was tolerated by the feudal Okayama lord, as it did not threaten his power or conflict greatly with the religious beliefs already practiced in the area.
By the time of the [[Meiji Restoration]] in 1868, the sect had gained followers in Kyushu and western and southwestern Honshū, all the way up to Tokyo. It gained independence from the Board of Shinto Affairs in 1876, and established its own Shinto shrine, the [[Munetada Shrine]], in Okayama in 1885. As of 1978, the group claimed 218,000 followers.

{{short description|Japanese new religion largely derived from Shinto roots}} [[File:黒住教本部.jpeg|thumb|right|Kurozumikyō headquarters in [[Okayama]]]] '''Kurozumikyō''' (黒住教), literally "the Teachings of Kurozumi," is a [[Japanese new religions|Japanese new religion]] largely derived from [[Shinto]] roots and founded in 1846.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Marukoto: The Teaching of Roundness |url=https://kurozumikyo.com/marukoto_e.html#history |access-date=2023-03-16 |website=kurozumikyo.com}}</ref> The founder, a Shinto priest by the name of Kurozumi, is claimed to have had a Divine union with [[Amaterasu]], Goddess of the sun and chief ''[[Kami]]'' in the Shinto [[Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]], in 1814.
The core beliefs of the sect center around this idea, and the assertion that Amaterasu is the source of all light and life, and [[Creator deity|creator]] of all the universe. Mankind is believed to be able to tap into the divine power of Amaterasu in order to heal the sick and perform other miracles. The sect is based in [[Okayama]], and focuses on the virtues of sincerity, selflessness, hard work, and affirmation of the established social order.
Though Kurozumi's divine experience occurred in 1814, the sect was not formally organized until 1846, when the priest and senior disciples assembled the ''Osadamegaki'', putting into writing all the beliefs, values, and laws of the sect. Originally, their religious and missionary activity was tolerated by the feudal Okayama lord, as it did not threaten his power or conflict greatly with the religious beliefs already practiced in the area.
By the time of the [[Meiji Restoration]] in 1868, the sect had gained followers in Kyushu and western and southwestern Honshū, all the way up to Tokyo. It gained independence from the Board of Shinto Affairs in 1876, and established its own Shinto shrine, the [[Munetada Shrine]], in Okayama in 1885. As of 1978, the group claimed 218,000 followers.
Muneharu Kurozumi, the Sixth Chief Patriarch of Kurozumikyō, is affiliated with the openly [[Historical revisionism (negationism)|revisionist]] lobby [[Nippon Kaigi]].<ref>Nippon Kaigi website</ref>
Today, Kurozumikyō Honbu (黒住教本部), the headquarters of the religion, is located on the southeastern summit of Shinto-zan (神道山) in [[Okayama]].
==References== {{reflist}} {{no footnotes|date=December 2010}} * Murakami Shigeyoshi (1985). "Kurozumikyō." ''Kōdansha Encyclopedia of Japan''. Tokyo: Kōdansha Ltd. * {{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Kurozumikyo and the New Religions of Japan |date=1988 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=0691020485 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=veE9DwAAQBAJ}}

The core beliefs of the sect center around this idea, and the assertion that Amaterasu is the source of all light and life, and [[Creator deity|creator]] of all the universe. Mankind is believed to be able to tap into the divine power of Amaterasu in order to heal the sick and perform other miracles. The sect is based in [[Okayama]], and focuses on the virtues of sincerity, selflessness, hard work, and affirmation of the established social order.
Though Kurozumi's divine experience occurred in 1814, the sect was not formally organized until 1846, when the priest and senior disciples assembled the ''Osadamegaki'', putting into writing all the beliefs, values, and laws of the sect. Originally, their religious and missionary activity was tolerated by the feudal Okayama lord, as it did not threaten his power or conflict greatly with the religious beliefs already practiced in the area.
By the time of the [[Meiji Restoration]] in 1868, the sect had gained followers in Kyushu and western and southwestern Honshū, all the way up to Tokyo. It gained independence from the Board of Shinto Affairs in 1876, and established its own Shinto shrine, the [[Munetada Shrine]], in Okayama in 1885. As of 1978, the group claimed 218,000 followers.
Muneharu Kurozumi, the Sixth Chief Patriarch of Kurozumikyō, is affiliated with the openly [[Historical revisionism (negationism)|revisionist]] lobby [[Nippon Kaigi]].<ref>Nippon Kaigi website</ref>
Today, Kurozumikyō Honbu (黒住教本部), the headquarters of the religion, is located on the southeastern summit of Shinto-zan (神道山) in [[Okayama]].

[[Category:Pages without translation templates]] <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Kurozumikyō}} [[de:Kurozumikyō]] [[fr:Kurozumikyō]] [[ja:黒住教]] [[pl:Kurozumi-kyō]]

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{short description|Japanese new religion largely derived from Shinto roots}} [[File:黒住教本部.jpeg|thumb|right|Kurozumikyō headquarters in [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]]]] '''Kurozumikyō''' (黒住教), literally "the Teachings of Kurozumi," is a [[:en:Japanese new religions|Japanese new religion]] largely derived from [[Shinto]] roots and founded in 1846.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Marukoto: The Teaching of Roundness |url=https://kurozumikyo.com/marukoto_e.html#history |access-date=2023-03-16 |website=kurozumikyo.com}}</ref> The founder, a Shinto priest by the name of Munetada Kurozumi ([[:ja:黒住宗忠|黒住宗忠]]), is claimed to have had a Divine union with [[Amaterasu]], Goddess of the sun and chief ''[[Kami]]'' in the Shinto [[Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]], in 1814.
The core beliefs of the sect center around this idea, and the assertion that Amaterasu is the source of all light and life, and [[:en:Creator deity|creator]] of all the universe. Mankind is believed to be able to tap into the divine power of Amaterasu in order to heal the sick and perform other miracles. The sect is based in [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]], and focuses on the virtues of sincerity, selflessness, hard work, and affirmation of the established social order.
Though Kurozumi's divine experience occurred in 1814, the sect was not formally organized until 1846, when the priest and senior disciples assembled the ''Osadamegaki'', putting into writing all the beliefs, values, and laws of the sect. Originally, their religious and missionary activity was tolerated by the feudal Okayama lord, as it did not threaten his power or conflict greatly with the religious beliefs already practiced in the area.
By the time of the [[Meiji Restoration]] in 1868, the sect had gained followers in Kyushu and western and southwestern Honshū, all the way up to Tokyo. It gained independence from the Board of Shinto Affairs in 1876, and established its own Shinto shrine, the [[Munetada Shrine]], in Okayama in 1885. As of 1978, the group claimed 218,000 followers.
Muneharu Kurozumi, the Sixth Chief Patriarch of Kurozumikyō, is affiliated with the openly [[:en:Historical revisionism (negationism)|revisionist]] lobby [[:en:Nippon Kaigi|Nippon Kaigi]].<ref>Nippon Kaigi website</ref>
Today, Kurozumikyō Honbu (黒住教本部), the headquarters of the religion, is located on the southeastern summit of Shinto-zan (神道山) in [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]].
==References== {{reflist}} {{no footnotes|date=December 2010}} * Murakami Shigeyoshi (1985). "Kurozumikyō." ''Kōdansha Encyclopedia of Japan''. Tokyo: Kōdansha Ltd. * {{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Kurozumikyo and the New Religions of Japan |date=1988 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=0691020485 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=veE9DwAAQBAJ}}
==External links== * [http://www.kurozumikyo.com/marukoto_e.html Marukoto: The Teaching of Roundness]

Originally, their religious and missionary activity was tolerated by the feudal Okayama lord, as it did not threaten his power or conflict greatly with the religious beliefs already practiced in the area.
By the time of the [[Meiji Restoration]] in 1868, the sect had gained followers in Kyushu and western and southwestern Honshū, all the way up to Tokyo. It gained independence from the Board of Shinto Affairs in 1876, and established its own Shinto shrine, the [[Munetada Shrine]], in Okayama in 1885. As of 1978, the group claimed 218,000 followers.
Muneharu Kurozumi, the Sixth Chief Patriarch of Kurozumikyō, is affiliated with the openly [[:en:Historical revisionism (negationism)|revisionist]] lobby [[:en:Nippon Kaigi|Nippon Kaigi]].<ref>Nippon Kaigi website</ref>
Today, Kurozumikyō Honbu (黒住教本部), the headquarters of the religion, is located on the southeastern summit of Shinto-zan (神道山) in [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kurozumikyō]][[de:Kurozumikyō]][[en:Kurozumikyō]][[fr:Kurozumikyō]][[ja:黒住教]][[pl:Kurozumi-kyō]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1793172]] Wikidata name: Kurozumikyō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Japanese new religion largely derived from Shinto roots}} [[File:黒住教本部.jpeg|thumb|right|Kurozumikyō headquarters in [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]]]] '''Kurozumikyō''' (黒住教), literally "the Teachings of Kurozumi," is a [[:en:Japanese new religions|Japanese new religion]] largely derived from [[Shinto]] roots and founded in 1846.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Marukoto: The Teaching of Roundness |url=https://kurozumikyo.com/marukoto_e.html#history |access-date=2023-03-16 |website=kurozumikyo.com}}</ref> The founder, a Shinto priest by the name of Munetada Kurozumi ([[:ja:黒住宗忠|黒住宗忠]]), is claimed to have had a Divine union with [[Amaterasu]], Goddess of the sun and chief ''[[Kami]]'' in the Shinto [[:en:Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]], in 1814.
The core beliefs of the sect center around this idea, and the assertion that Amaterasu is the source of all light and life, and [[:en:Creator deity|creator]] of all the universe. Mankind is believed to be able to tap into the divine power of Amaterasu in order to heal the sick and perform other miracles. The sect is based in [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]], and focuses on the virtues of sincerity, selflessness, hard work, and affirmation of the established social order.
Though Kurozumi's divine experience occurred in 1814, the sect was not formally organized until 1846, when the priest and senior disciples assembled the ''Osadamegaki'', putting into writing all the beliefs, values, and laws of the sect. Originally, their religious and missionary activity was tolerated by the feudal Okayama lord, as it did not threaten his power or conflict greatly with the religious beliefs already practiced in the area.
By the time of the [[Meiji Restoration]] in 1868, the sect had gained followers in Kyushu and western and southwestern Honshū, all the way up to Tokyo. It gained independence from the Board of Shinto Affairs in 1876, and established its own Shinto shrine, the [[Munetada Shrine]], in Okayama in 1885. As of 1978, the group claimed 218,000 followers.
Muneharu Kurozumi, the Sixth Chief Patriarch of Kurozumikyō, is affiliated with the openly [[:en:Historical revisionism (negationism)|revisionist]] lobby [[:en:Nippon Kaigi|Nippon Kaigi]].<ref>Nippon Kaigi website</ref>
Today, Kurozumikyō Honbu (黒住教本部), the headquarters of the religion, is located on the southeastern summit of Shinto-zan (神道山) in [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]].
==References== {{reflist}} {{no footnotes|date=December 2010}} * Murakami Shigeyoshi (1985). "Kurozumikyō." ''Kōdansha Encyclopedia of Japan''. Tokyo: Kōdansha Ltd. * {{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Kurozumikyo and the New Religions of Japan |date=1988 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=0691020485 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=veE9DwAAQBAJ}}
==External links== * [http://www.kurozumikyo.com/marukoto_e.html Marukoto: The Teaching of Roundness]

Mankind is believed to be able to tap into the divine power of Amaterasu in order to heal the sick and perform other miracles. Originally, their religious and missionary activity was tolerated by the feudal Okayama lord, as it did not threaten his power or conflict greatly with the religious beliefs already practiced in the area.

It gained independence from the Board of Shinto Affairs in 1876, and established its own Shinto shrine, the [[Munetada Shrine]], in Okayama in 1885. As of 1978, the group claimed 218,000 followers.
Muneharu Kurozumi, the Sixth Chief Patriarch of Kurozumikyō, is affiliated with the openly [[:en:Historical revisionism (negationism)|revisionist]] lobby [[:en:Nippon Kaigi|Nippon Kaigi]].<ref>Nippon Kaigi website</ref>
Today, Kurozumikyō Honbu (黒住教本部), the headquarters of the religion, is located on the southeastern summit of Shinto-zan (神道山) in [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kurozumikyō]][[de:Kurozumikyō]][[en:Kurozumikyō]][[fr:Kurozumikyō]][[ja:黒住教]][[pl:Kurozumi-kyō]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kurozumikyō]][[de:Kurozumikyō]][[en:Kurozumikyō]][[fr:Kurozumikyō]][[ja:黒住教]][[pl:Kurozumi-kyō]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kurozumikyō]][[de:Kurozumikyō]][[en:Kurozumikyō]][[fr:Kurozumikyō]][[ja:黒住教]][[pl:Kurozumi-kyō]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1793172]] Wikidata name: Kurozumikyō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Japanese new religion largely derived from Shinto roots}} [[File:黒住教本部.jpeg|thumb|right|Kurozumikyō headquarters in {{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|WD=Q200078|lt=Okayama}}]] '''Kurozumikyō''' (黒住教), literally "the Teachings of Kurozumi," is a {{ill|Japanese new religions|en|Japanese new religions|WD=Q1263926|lt=Japanese new religion}} largely derived from [[Shinto]] roots and founded in 1846.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Marukoto: The Teaching of Roundness |url=https://kurozumikyo.com/marukoto_e.html#history |access-date=2023-03-16 |website=kurozumikyo.com}}</ref> The founder, a Shinto priest by the name of Munetada Kurozumi ([[:ja:黒住宗忠|黒住宗忠]]), is claimed to have had a Divine union with [[Amaterasu]], Goddess of the sun and chief ''[[Kami]]'' in the Shinto [[:en:Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]], in 1814.
The core beliefs of the sect center around this idea, and the assertion that Amaterasu is the source of all light and life, and {{ill|Creator deity|en|Creator deity|WD=Q635155|lt=creator}} of all the universe. Mankind is believed to be able to tap into the divine power of Amaterasu in order to heal the sick and perform other miracles. The sect is based in {{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|WD=Q200078|lt=Okayama}}, and focuses on the virtues of sincerity, selflessness, hard work, and affirmation of the established social order.
Though Kurozumi's divine experience occurred in 1814, the sect was not formally organized until 1846, when the priest and senior disciples assembled the ''Osadamegaki'', putting into writing all the beliefs, values, and laws of the sect. Originally, their religious and missionary activity was tolerated by the feudal Okayama lord, as it did not threaten his power or conflict greatly with the religious beliefs already practiced in the area.
By the time of the [[Meiji Restoration]] in 1868, the sect had gained followers in Kyushu and western and southwestern Honshū, all the way up to Tokyo. It gained independence from the Board of Shinto Affairs in 1876, and established its own Shinto shrine, the [[Munetada Shrine]], in Okayama in 1885. As of 1978, the group claimed 218,000 followers.
Muneharu Kurozumi, the Sixth Chief Patriarch of Kurozumikyō, is affiliated with the openly [[:en:Historical revisionism (negationism)|revisionist]] lobby {{ill|Nippon Kaigi|en|Nippon Kaigi|WD=Q11507216|lt=Nippon Kaigi}}.<ref>Nippon Kaigi website</ref>
Today, Kurozumikyō Honbu (黒住教本部), the headquarters of the religion, is located on the southeastern summit of Shinto-zan (神道山) in {{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|WD=Q200078|lt=Okayama}}.
==References== {{reflist}} {{no footnotes|date=December 2010}} * Murakami Shigeyoshi (1985). "Kurozumikyō." ''Kōdansha Encyclopedia of Japan''. Tokyo: Kōdansha Ltd. * {{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Kurozumikyo and the New Religions of Japan |date=1988 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=0691020485 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=veE9DwAAQBAJ}}
==External links== * [http://www.kurozumikyo.com/marukoto_e.html Marukoto: The Teaching of Roundness]
{{Sect Shinto}} {{Authority control}}

The core beliefs of the sect center around this idea, and the assertion that Amaterasu is the source of all light and life, and {{ill|Creator deity|en|Creator deity|WD=Q635155|lt=creator}} of all the universe. Mankind is believed to be able to tap into the divine power of Amaterasu in order to heal the sick and perform other miracles. The sect is based in {{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|WD=Q200078|lt=Okayama}}, and focuses on the virtues of sincerity, selflessness, hard work, and affirmation of the established social order.
Though Kurozumi's divine experience occurred in 1814, the sect was not formally organized until 1846, when the priest and senior disciples assembled the ''Osadamegaki'', putting into writing all the beliefs, values, and laws of the sect. Originally, their religious and missionary activity was tolerated by the feudal Okayama lord, as it did not threaten his power or conflict greatly with the religious beliefs already practiced in the area.
By the time of the [[Meiji Restoration]] in 1868, the sect had gained followers in Kyushu and western and southwestern Honshū, all the way up to Tokyo. It gained independence from the Board of Shinto Affairs in 1876, and established its own Shinto shrine, the [[Munetada Shrine]], in Okayama in 1885. As of 1978, the group claimed 218,000 followers.
Muneharu Kurozumi, the Sixth Chief Patriarch of Kurozumikyō, is affiliated with the openly [[:en:Historical revisionism (negationism)|revisionist]] lobby {{ill|Nippon Kaigi|en|Nippon Kaigi|WD=Q11507216|lt=Nippon Kaigi}}.<ref>Nippon Kaigi website</ref>
Today, Kurozumikyō Honbu (黒住教本部), the headquarters of the religion, is located on the southeastern summit of Shinto-zan (神道山) in {{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|WD=Q200078|lt=Okayama}}.
==References== {{reflist}} {{no footnotes|date=December 2010}} * Murakami Shigeyoshi (1985). "Kurozumikyō." ''Kōdansha Encyclopedia of Japan''. Tokyo: Kōdansha Ltd. * {{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Kurozumikyo and the New Religions of Japan |date=1988 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=0691020485 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=veE9DwAAQBAJ}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kurozumikyō]][[de:Kurozumikyō]][[en:Kurozumikyō]][[fr:Kurozumikyō]][[ja:黒住教]][[pl:Kurozumi-kyō]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kurozumikyō]][[de:Kurozumikyō]][[en:Kurozumikyō]][[fr:Kurozumikyō]][[ja:黒住教]][[pl:Kurozumi-kyō]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Kurozumikyō]][[en:Kurozumikyō]][[fr:Kurozumikyō]][[ja:黒住教]][[pl:Kurozumi-kyō]]
{{short description|Japanese new religion largely derived from Shinto roots}} [[File:黒住教本部.jpeg|thumb|right|Kurozumikyō headquarters in {{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|qid=Q200078|lt=Okayama|1=Okayama}}]] '''Kurozumikyō''' (黒住教), literally "the Teachings of Kurozumi," is a {{ill|Japanese new religions|en|Japanese new religions|qid=Q1263926|lt=Japanese new religion|1=Japanese new religions}} largely derived from [[Shinto]] roots and founded in 1846.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Marukoto: The Teaching of Roundness |url=https://kurozumikyo.com/marukoto_e.html#history |access-date=2023-03-16 |website=kurozumikyo.com}}</ref> The founder, a Shinto priest by the name of Munetada Kurozumi ([[:ja:黒住宗忠|黒住宗忠]]), is claimed to have had a Divine union with [[Amaterasu]], Goddess of the sun and chief ''[[Kami]]'' in the Shinto [[:en:Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]], in 1814.
The core beliefs of the sect center around this idea, and the assertion that Amaterasu is the source of all light and life, and {{ill|Creator deity|en|Creator deity|qid=Q635155|lt=creator|1=Creator deity}} of all the universe. Mankind is believed to be able to tap into the divine power of Amaterasu in order to heal the sick and perform other miracles. The sect is based in {{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|qid=Q200078|lt=Okayama|1=Okayama}}, and focuses on the virtues of sincerity, selflessness, hard work, and affirmation of the established social order.
Though Kurozumi's divine experience occurred in 1814, the sect was not formally organized until 1846, when the priest and senior disciples assembled the ''Osadamegaki'', putting into writing all the beliefs, values, and laws of the sect. Originally, their religious and missionary activity was tolerated by the feudal Okayama lord, as it did not threaten his power or conflict greatly with the religious beliefs already practiced in the area.
By the time of the [[Meiji Restoration]] in 1868, the sect had gained followers in Kyushu and western and southwestern Honshū, all the way up to Tokyo. It gained independence from the Board of Shinto Affairs in 1876, and established its own Shinto shrine, the [[Munetada Shrine]], in Okayama in 1885. As of 1978, the group claimed 218,000 followers.
Muneharu Kurozumi, the Sixth Chief Patriarch of Kurozumikyō, is affiliated with the openly [[:en:Historical revisionism (negationism)|revisionist]] lobby {{ill|Nippon Kaigi|en|Nippon Kaigi|qid=Q11507216|lt=Nippon Kaigi|1=Nippon Kaigi}}.<ref>Nippon Kaigi website</ref>
Today, Kurozumikyō Honbu (黒住教本部), the headquarters of the religion, is located on the southeastern summit of Shinto-zan (神道山) in {{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|qid=Q200078|lt=Okayama|1=Okayama}}.
==References== {{reflist}} {{no footnotes|date=December 2010}} * Murakami Shigeyoshi (1985). "Kurozumikyō." ''Kōdansha Encyclopedia of Japan''. Tokyo: Kōdansha Ltd. * {{cite book |last1=Hardacre |first1=Helen |title=Kurozumikyo and the New Religions of Japan |date=1988 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=0691020485 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=veE9DwAAQBAJ}}
==External links== * [http://www.kurozumikyo.com/marukoto_e.html Marukoto: The Teaching of Roundness]
